  8. 3:03:10 AM — SAP S/4HANA creates work order Priority: High Task: Inspect cooling fins + oil level + load tap changer
  9. 3:20 AM — Field technician receives job on mobile Drives to site before failure window.
  10. 4:05 AM — Technician finds blocked cooling fins Dust + debris + vegetation restricting airflow.
  11. 4:40 AM — Issue cleared, temperature stabilizes Transformer returns to normal operating range.
  12. Failure avoided. Outage avoided. $40k avoided. And the entire chain is auditable inside S/4HANA.

What Example Proves (The Architectural Insight)

This single incident captures the entire purpose of architecture. The utility didn’t just detect a problem — it prevented failure because AI, events, workflows, and S/4HANA acted as one system. The model didn’t need to be perfect; it only needed to be early. Event Mesh eliminated latency. Build Process Automation added human judgment. S/4HANA ensured the action was auditable and compliant. And the field technician received the job before the asset crossed the failure threshold.

This is the difference between “AI that predicts” and AI that protects the grid.

entry.title: How to Set Up B2B Outbound Integration with Trading Partner Management in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
1. Introduction

If you work in integration long enough, you hear the same prediction again and again, EDI is going away. In reality, that has not happened. APIs and events have absolutely changed the landscape, but in many B2B scenarios, EDI is still very much alive. For a lot of companies, it remains one of the most important ways to exchange business documents with partners.

SAP’s B2B integration story has also changed quite a bit over the years. Many teams started with Seeburger on SAP XI/PI, then moved to the SAP B2B add-on on PO. In the cloud world, SAP Cloud Integration has gone through a few stages as well from basic converters, to Integration Advisor with MIGs and MAGs, and now to Trading Partner Management (TPM) together with B2B monitoring.

Because of that evolution, TPM can feel harder than it really is, especially when you first open it. It involved MIGs, agreements, identifiers, communication channels, templates, standard iFlows, custom mapping flows, and runtime processing. With so many changes over the years, it is understandable that EDI on Cloud Integration can seem complex at first.

That is exactly why I wanted to write this. Instead of starting with a complex multi-partner setup, I think it is much easier to begin with a simple outbound scenario and understand the core building blocks first. Once that mental model is clear, the rest of the setup becomes much more manageable.

In this post, I will focus on a basic outbound flow and walk through the main setup steps in SAP Integration Suite.

What “Outbound” means in TPM

In an outbound scenario, company initiates the message exchange:

  1. A business document is created in the backend, often as an IDoc from ERP or SAP S/4HANA.
  2. IDoc is sent to Cloud Integration.
  3. Cloud Integration transforms it into an EDI message, for example an X12 850
  4. Envelopes it into an interchange and delivers it to the trading partner.
  5. Optionally you can also support functional acknowledgements (for example, X12 997).

2.    End to End Setup Overview in Cloud Integration

When you look at TPM for the first time, one of the biggest questions is usually What needs to be done, and in what order?

For a basic outbound EDI setup, this is the sequence I typically follow:

  1. Create MIGs for IDoc, X12, or EDIFACT
  2. Create the required number ranges
  3. Deploy and configure the standard TPM iFlows
  4. Build the custom mapping iFlow
  5. Define your own company profile
    • identifiers
    • systems
    • communications
  6. Define the trading partner profile
    • identifiers
    • systems
    • communications
  7. Create agreement templates
  8. Create agreements and B2B scenarios
  9. Perform end-to-end testing

There are a lot of individual objects in that list, but once you work through them in sequence, the setup starts to make sense.

3.    Integration Advisor

Before you get into agreements, routing, or acknowledgements, you need the message structures in place. In SAP Integration Suite, that starts with Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs).

3.1 Create MIGs for IDoc / X12 / EDIFACT

MIGs matter because they act as the structural definition for the messages TPM works with. They are used in validation, routing, and transaction configuration.

3.1.1  Steps to Create Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs) for IDoc

To create an IDoc MIG:

  1. Navigate to Design → MIGs.

     

  2. Create a new MIG

     

  3. Choose the standard type system for SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc. Search for the message type you need, for example ORDERS.ORDERS05. Select the appropriate version, such as S/4HANA 1709. You can skip uploading a payload if you do not need it for this step.

     

     

     

     

  4. When naming the MIG, make sure the direction fits your use case:

    • IN = the MIG is used as the source
    • OUT = the MIG is used as the target
    • BOTH = the same MIG may be used in either direction

       

  5. Under Own Business Context, select the relevant Country/Region. Then click on Create.
    Examples:
    X12: United States
    EDIFACT: United Kingdom
    IDoc: United States and United Kingdom (as applicable)

     

     

  6. Right-click the node, select required fields, then Save and Activate.

     

     

3.1.2 Steps to Create Message Implementation Guidelines (MIGs) for X12

In this example, the outbound IDoc is mapped to X12 850 version 5010. That means you also need a second MIG that represents the X12 target structure.

The process is basically the same as the IDoc MIG creation. The main point is that both the source and target structures are defined clearly before you continue.

 

 

4 Cloud Integration

4.1   Deploy Standard Trading Partner Management Iflows

  1. Navigate to Discover → Integrations.

     

  2. Search for “Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2”. Select the package, then click the Copy button in the top-right corner.

     

     

  3. Go to Design → Integration & APIs. Search for “Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2”, select the package, and deploy the required integration flows.
  4. Depending on the connectivity setup, deploy the following standard content as-is (no changes):
    Reusable Groovy Scripts V2
    Step 1 - Sender AS2 Communication Flow V2
    Step 1 - Sender IDOC Communication Flow V2
    Step 1 - Sender Process Direct Communication Flow V2
    Step 1b - Write Message to Message Queue
    Step 2 - Interchange Processing Flow V2
    Step 3 - Receiver Communication Flow V2
    Note: Based on your connectivity model, only the relevant Step 1 sender flow needs to be deployed. However, the following are mandatory in all cases:
    • Reusable Groovy Scripts
    • Step 1b
    • Step 2
    • Step 3

    These iFlows provide the standard runtime pipeline used by TPM for routing, enveloping, and message delivery.

4.2 Create Custom Mapping Flow

In Cloud Integration, there are two main transformation approaches:

  • Message Mapping
  • Mapping Guidelines (MAG)

In this scenario, we use Message Mapping to convert data from IDoc to EDI and vice versa.

So the custom iFlow is responsible for transforming the IDoc into the target EDI structure. TPM then calls that iFlow at runtime through a Process Direct endpoint. When you configure the agreement later, you reference this custom mapping using a mapping exit.

A simple way to think about it is:

  • Standard TPM content = routing, interchange handling, delivery
  • Custom mapping iFlow = your document transformation logic

That separation helped me a lot when I was trying to understand where each piece belongs.

 

 

 

 

 

 

4.3  Create Number Ranges

Next, create the number ranges required for interchange or transaction control.

 

 

Create 5a number range and provide the following details:

  1. Name: Number range name as referenced in Trading Partner Management
  2. Description
  3. Minimum Value
  4. Maximum Value
  5. Field Length: If set to 0, the system derives the length from the maximum value
  6. Rotate: Indicates whether numbering should restart from the minimum value after the maximum is reached

If you do not want a separate number range for every partner, you can also create default number ranges and reuse them across multiple agreements.

 

 

5. Trading Partner Management

5.1  Define “Self” Company Profile in Trading Partner Management

5.1.1 Create Company Profile

Create a new company profile for your own organization, for example SELF_COMPANY.

Note: Only one company profile can exist per tenant.

 

 

 

5.1.2  Create Identifiers (for Self)

You can maintain multiple identifiers for your self-company profile. In this example, we create two:

  • ERP Identifier– DR1CLNT100 (SNDPRN)
    • Identification: DR1CLNT100
    • Alias: DR1CLNT100
    • Type System: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
    • Scheme: N/A

       

  • EDI Identifier – SELF_1234
    • Identification: SELF_1234
    • Alias: SELF_1234
    • Type System: ASC X12
    • Scheme: ZZ

       

5.1.3 Create Systems (for Self)

Under the Systems tab, create the backend system definition.

The naming is flexible, but I would recommend keeping it consistent and easy to recognize, especially once you have multiple landscapes. 

In this example:

  • Name: S4
  • Alias: S4
  • Type: Create New in this case
    • Name: S4
    • Description: S4
    • Deployment Type: OnPremise
    • Application: S4
  • Purpose: Dev

     

     

     

5.1.3.1  Create Type Systems ( for Self)

Create the relevant type system, for example:

  • Name: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
  • Version: your SAP S/4HANA release

If you are not sure which version to use, the important thing is that it aligns with the DOCREL value in the IDoc control record EDI_DC40.

 

 

 

5.1.3.1  Create Communications

Since SAP S/4HANA is sending the IDoc into Cloud Integration, create a sender communication channel such as:

  • Name: SND_IDOC
  • Alias: SND_IDOC
  • Direction: Sender
  • Adapter: IDOC

     

5.2  Define Trading Partner in Trading Partner Management

After your self-company is ready, create the external partner profile.

5.2.1  Create Trading Partners

Go to Design → B2B Scenarios. Under the Partner Profile tab, choose Create Profile.

Example:

  • Partner Name: PARTNER1
  • Company Short Name: PARTNER1

     

     

5.2.2 Create Identifiers (for Trading Partner)

You can maintain multiple identifiers for each trading partner. In this example, we create two:

  • ERP Identifier– PARTNER1(RCVPRN)
    • Identification: PARTNER1
    • Alias: PARTNER1
    • Type System: SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc
    • Scheme: N/A

       

  • EDI Identifier – PART_123
    • Identification: PART_123
    • Alias: PART_123
    • Type System: ASC X12
    • Scheme: ZZ

       

5.2.3  Create System (for Trading Partner)

Create a system entry for the trading partner. This represents the external side of the exchange and is later connected to the communication details.

Create a System for Trading Partner for PARTNER1.

 

 

 

 

5.2.3.1  Create Type System (for Trading Partner)

Create a Type System for the Trading Partner with the Below details

  • Name: ASC X12
  • Type: 005010

     

5.2.3.2   Create Communications (for Trading Partner)

Since the trading partner receives the outbound EDI from Cloud Integration, create a receiver communication channel.

  • Name: RCV_PD
  • Alias: RCV_PD
  • Direction: Receiver
  • Adapter: ProcessDirect
  • Address: /PD

     

5.3 Agreement Template

Agreement templates are optional, but they become very useful once you start scaling.

If you only have one partner and one transaction, they may feel like extra setup. But if you expect multiple partners or multiple message types, templates help you standardize the overall agreement structure and reduce repeated configuration.

 

 

 

 

5.4 Agreements

Agreements are where the relationship between your company, the partner, and the business transaction is actually defined.

To create one:

  • Go to the Agreement tab and click on create.

     

  • Select the agreement template, for example SAP_PARTNER

     

  • Choose Copy from Template

     

  • Select the trading partner

     

  • Open the draft agreement

     

This is the point where TPM starts to feel much more concrete, because now you are tying the profiles, message structures, mappings, and communication setup together.

5.4.1 B2B Scenarios

Inside the agreement, create the actual B2B scenario.

Recommended example:

  • Name: PARTNER1_ORDERS_ORDERS05_To_850_5010
  • Business Transaction Pattern: One way notification
  • For an outbound scenario, Set your company role to Initiator (For an inbound scenario, Set your company role to Reactor) 

     

     

After creating the transaction:

  1. Select the Sender Communication Channel

     

  2. Select the Sender Interchange and assign the appropriate MIG

     

  3. Maintain the Mapping Process Direct adapter address

     

  4. Configure the Receiver Interchange
    • Select the appropriate MIG
    • Assign the number range
    • Enable functional acknowledgement if required
    • Maintain custom separators if needed

       

  5. Enter the Receiver Communication Channel

Repeat the same process for any additional transactions required for other message types or partners.

Finally, save and activate the agreement.

 

 

In my view, this is the most important part to understand conceptually: the agreement is where TPM becomes executable. Until the agreement is active, the rest of the setup is mostly just preparation.

5.5 Perform End To End Testing

Once the following setups are in place, you are ready for your first end to end test:

  • MIGs are created and activated
  • Standard TPM iFlows are deployed
  • Self company and trading partner profiles are maintained
  • Agreement is configured and activated
  • Custom mapping iFlow is available through ProcessDirect

At this point, you can send a test IDoc from the backend, let Cloud Integration process and transform it into EDI, and verify that the message is successfully routed and delivered to the trading partner.

 

 

 

Final thoughts

When I first started working with Trading Partner Management, it seemed more complex than I expected. There are many setups involved, and at the beginning it is not always obvious what is essential and what can wait. But once I worked through a simple outbound scenario, the overall design started to make much more sense.

What helped me most was thinking about TPM in three parts:

  • message structure with MIGs
  • runtime orchestration with the standard TPM flows
  • business transformation with the custom mapping iFlow

Once those three pieces are clear, TPM becomes much easier to approach.

If you are just getting started, I would recommend building one simple outbound scenario end to end before moving on to multiple partners, multiple message types, or reusable templates. That first working flow gives you a solid foundation for the rest of the setup.

entry.title: Enforcing Package-Level Segregation in a Shared SAP Cloud Integration Tenant
entry.content:
The Problem Nobody Talks About

Shared SAP Integration Suite tenants are more common than SAP's documentation assumes. Whether the reason is cost optimization, organizational structure, or a multi-subsidiary model, many teams end up with developers and operators from different organizations working on the same Cloud Integration tenant.

At some point, someone asks the question:

How do we make sure our developers cannot accidentally touch another organization's integration flows?

You open the SAP Help Portal and review the standard role collections: PI_Integration_Developer, PI_Administrator, PI_Business_Expert, PI_Read_Only. They are well designed and aligned with common personas — but they are tenant-wide by default. Assigning one of them gives the user access across the entire tenant, not just to a specific organization's packages.

That gap is what this article addresses.

Why Standard Role Collections Are Not Enough

SAP's standard role collections answer one question well:

What can this user do?

In a shared tenant, there is a second question that matters just as much:

On which packages can they do it?

Without an answer to the second question, you expose yourself to real risks:

  • A developer from Organization A can edit or redeploy integration flows belonging to Organization B.
  • An operator from Organization A can configure and deploy Organization B artifacts.
  • A tracing user from Organization A can inspect message payloads from Organization B processes, potentially exposing sensitive business data.

The solution is not to replace SAP's authorization model. It is to extend it correctly.

The Architecture: Two Layers of Control

The model combines two mechanisms that work independently but must be used together.

Layer 1 — Role Collections: what the user can do

Custom role collections in SAP BTP define the functional permissions: configure parameters, deploy artifacts, monitor logs, edit integration flows, manage security materials, view payloads. This is standard BTP role management, and it answers the first question.

Layer 2 — Access Policies: which packages those actions apply to

Access Policies in SAP Cloud Integration restrict the scope of the functional permissions to a specific set of integration packages. A user may have permission to configure and deploy — but only for the packages explicitly listed in the corresponding Access Policy.

The link between the two layers is a custom role created from CustomRoleTemplate in BTP. This custom role carries a static attribute (custom_role) whose value must match the Access Policy role name in Cloud Integration exactly. That value is the binding key between both layers.

Both layers are required:

  • Role collections without Access Policies remain tenant-wide.
  • Access Policies without the matching custom role are not enforced.
  • A mismatch in the attribute value — including case differences — breaks the binding silently.

Diagram 1 — Two-Layer Architecture

How the Authorization Check Works

Understanding the enforcement mechanism helps you design and troubleshoot the model correctly.

When a user attempts to act on an integration package — configure, deploy, edit, trace — Cloud Integration performs a two-level check:

Level 1 — Task check Does the user's role collection include the role template required for this action? The functional scopes are evaluated from the user's XSUAA token. If WorkspacePackagesConfigure is absent, access is denied regardless of anything else.

Level 2 — Scope check Does the user have a custom role whose custom_role attribute matches an Access Policy that references this package? The runtime resolves the attribute at call time and compares it against the registered Access Policy role names. If no matching policy covers the target package, access is denied even if the user has the functional role template.

Both checks must pass independently. The practical consequence:

  • Functional role without a matching Access Policy custom role → blocked on every protected package.
  • Custom role without the functional role template → blocked on every action regardless of package.
  • Custom role present, but the target package not listed in the Access Policy → blocked on that specific package.

This is genuine platform-level enforcement, not just UI restrictions.

What You Need to Know Before You Start

These are the behaviors that are easy to miss and hard to diagnose when you get them wrong.

Role collections are scoped per subaccount. A role collection created in DEV does not exist in PRD. Each subaccount where the shared tenant is consumed requires its own set of role collections and custom roles. Access Policies, by contrast, live inside the Cloud Integration tenant and are shared across subaccounts.

Create the role collection before the custom role. The BTP wizard for creating a custom role requires you to assign it to an existing role collection. If the target collection does not exist yet, you cannot complete the wizard. The recommended order is: role collection first, then custom role, then Access Policy.

The attribute value must be confirmed as a tag. When configuring the custom_role attribute in the custom role wizard, typing the value is not enough. You must press Enter to confirm it as a tag. The Next button will not activate until this is done. This is easy to overlook and produces no error message.

PI_Business_Expert is a role collection, not a role template. If you need tracing and payload visibility in a custom role collection, you cannot nest PI_Business_Expert inside it — SAP BTP does not support nesting role collections. The capability you need is the underlying role template AuthGroup_BusinessExpert (it!bXX). And critically: without combining it with your organization's custom role, payload visibility applies across the entire tenant.

Package references are not dynamic. Access Policies do not auto-discover packages. Each integration package must be added explicitly as an artifact reference (Type: Integration Package, Condition: Equals, Value: exact package name). A naming convention helps with governance, but it does not automatically protect new packages — that requires an operational process.

Diagram 2 — Creation Order

Implementing the Pattern

The implementation follows three steps. The SAP Help Portal documents the role templates and Access Policy configuration in detail; the focus here is on the design decisions and sequencing that matter in a shared tenant context.

Step 1 — Define your profiles and their functional scope

Before touching BTP, decide what each organizational profile needs to do. A typical setup for a production environment includes at least:

  • An operator profile — configure parameters, deploy artifacts, monitor logs. No iFlow edit access.
  • A tracing profile — payload visibility and trace activation, kept separate from the operator profile so it can be assigned temporarily during incident investigation and revoked afterward.
  • An administrator profile — full design-time access including iFlow edit, transport, and Access Policy management.

For non-production environments, a developer profile with full design-time capabilities is added in the DEV subaccount only.

Each profile becomes a custom role collection. The functional role templates for each profile are documented in SAP Help — Tasks and Permissions.

Step 2 — Create the custom role from CustomRoleTemplate

In BTP Cockpit, navigate to Security → Roles and search for CustomRoleTemplate under the Cloud Integration runtime application (identifier it!bXX — the exact value is tenant-specific, verify it in your subaccount).

Create a custom role with:

  • A descriptive name (e.g., ORGA_PI_PackageAccess)
  • Attribute: custom_role, Source: Static, Value: same as the role name
  • Assigned to the first role collection you created

Then add this custom role to every additional role collection that must be scoped to the same organization's packages. Any role collection missing the custom role operates without scope restriction.

Step 3 — Create the Access Policy in Cloud Integration

In Integration Suite, navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Manage Security → Access Policies.

Create a policy with a Role Name that matches the custom role attribute value exactly. Then add one artifact reference per integration package owned by the organization:

  • Type: Integration Package
  • Attribute: Name
  • Condition: Equals
  • Value: exact package name as shown in Cloud Integration

No wildcard or regex matching is available for this combination — each package must be listed individually.

Diagram 3 — Shared Tenant Model

Keeping the Model Consistent Over Time

The initial setup is the easy part. The harder part is operational discipline around new packages.

Every time a new integration package is created, it must be explicitly added to the corresponding Access Policy. Until it is, the package is unprotected — any user with the right functional role templates can act on it, regardless of which organization owns it.

A practical governance process:

  1. Enforce a naming convention (e.g., ORGA_*) so package ownership is unambiguous.
  2. Add the package to the Access Policy as part of the package creation checklist — before any iFlow is deployed into it.
  3. No changes to custom roles or role collections are required for new packages.

The naming convention does not enforce protection automatically. It makes the governance process auditable.

Conclusion

The combination of custom role collections and Access Policies gives you genuine package-level segregation in a shared SAP Cloud Integration tenant — enforced at the platform level, not just through UI restrictions.

The model has a deliberate boundary: it covers Cloud Integration (CPI) design-time and runtime access. API Management — API proxies, API products, Developer Hub permissions — has a different authorization model and requires a separate governance approach.

The two things that determine whether this model holds up in practice are not technical: they are the operational process for new packages, and the governance over who holds role collections that include AccessPoliciesEdit. The platform enforces the rules you define. Defining them consistently is your responsibility.

AI disclosure: AI tools were used for language tuning and structure. The technical content, implementation decisions, and validation were performed by the author on a live SAP Integration Suite tenant running Integration Suite 4.7.0 and Cloud Integration Runtime 8.40.25.

References

  • SAP Help — Managing Access Policies (Cloud Foundry)
  • SAP KBA 3223361 — Configure and deploy without edit access in #SAP-Cloud-Integration
  • SAP KBA 3270689 — Creating a customized role for #SAP-Cloud-Integration in Cloud Foundry
  • SAP Blog — Access Policies: Restrict Access to Integration Artifacts in #SAP-Integration-Suite
  • SAP Blog — Access Policies for Integration Packages (increment 2401+) in #SAP-Integration-Suite
  • SAP Blog — Strengthening SAP Cloud Integration Security in #SAP-Integration-Suite

entry.title: Enterprise-ready AI at scale: Securely connect and govern AI agents with SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
AI agents are quickly moving from concept to reality. The question is no longer whether organizations will adopt agentic AI. The question is how they can do it securely, responsibly, and at scale.

Join us for our upcoming webinar, "Enterprise-ready AI at scale: Securely connect and govern AI agents with SAP Integration Suite".

In an agentic AI landscape, integration becomes much more than connecting systems. It helps organizations understand how AI agents access business data, make decisions, interact with processes, and operate across complex enterprise environments.

During this session, we'll explore the latest AI-powered innovations in SAP Integration Suite and discuss how they help businesses securely connect, monitor, and govern AI agents while maintaining trusted business context.

If you work with customers on integration, architecture, AI, or digital transformation initiatives, this session will provide valuable insights and practical takeaways.

Register now and learn how SAP Integration Suite is helping organizations build a strong foundation for enterprise-ready AI.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/jun/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014439.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPIntegrationSuite #BusinessAI #AgenticAI #EnterpriseArchitecture #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: From Batch to Event-Driven: Liberating SFTP Data with Micro-Integrations
entry.content:
Introduction

At the end of last year, Micro-Integrations were introduced as a new capability within SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh.

This capability provides lightweight integration functionality to easily connect SaaS and legacy applications, LLMs and AI Agents, messaging services, data stores, etc. directly to your event brokers. It also supports basic transformations, comparable to message mappings.

The keyword here is lightweight. Micro-Integrations are not a replacement for Cloud Integration, it complements it. Cloud Integration is designed to handle integration complexity way beyond the level that the Micro-Integrations capability can handle. Micro-Integrations focus on simplicity, speed, and high throughput use cases.

In practice, using Cloud Integration for simple pass-through scenarios from or to Advanced Event Mesh with no transformation (apart from perhaps a simple message mapping) can be inefficient. In high-volume scenarios (e.g. Event Streaming) it means you’re consuming a lot of messages without leveraging the true strength of Cloud Integration.
This is exactly where the Micro-Integrations capability adds value: handling lightweight, high-throughput integrations, closer to the event broker, while leaving the more complex transformations to Cloud Integration.

In this blog, we will explore the SFTP Source Micro-Integration. It enables you to convert files into JSON messages, publish them to the event mesh, and make them available to your applications at scale in near real-time. From there, the data can be distributed across consuming systems in real time, eliminating the need for complex, polling-based point-to-point integrations.

This blog is structured into five main sections:

  • Introduction
  • The prerequisites
  • The scenario
  • The process
  • Create a 1K broker service in SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh
  • Create a temporary SFTP Server
  • Create the SFTP Source Micro-Integration
  • Configure the flow
  • Build the content-based topic
  • Testing the Micro-Integration
  • Troubleshooting
  • Final thoughts

 

The prerequisites

What you need to run through this scenario from A-Z

  1. Access to SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh, including the required roles:
    1. Mission Control Manager
    2. Micro-Integrations Manager
  1. Sufficient entitlements to create a 1K broker service in the SAP BTP subaccount where Advanced Event Mesh is hosted.
    To verify this:
    1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Subaccount
    2. Open “Entitlements” from the left-hand menu
    3. Scroll down to the service “integration-suite-advanced-event-mesh”
    4. You should see an available quota under the plan “broker-1K”.

  2. An SFTP client installed on your local workstation.
    In the blog I’m using FileZilla Client, but any equivalent tool should do the job.

The scenario

In this example, a retail chain with stores across EMEA has each point-of-sale (POS) system dropping a CSV file containing sales line items onto an SFTP server, folder:
/upload/retail/sales/inbound.
Every night, a batch job processes these files and loads the data into a data warehouse.

In this process, nothing is broken, but this data is valuable and the way it moves holds it hostage.

It’s not traveling in real-time, so inventory systems can’t react to a sell-out until tomorrow, the store dashboard only reflect yesterday’s data, fraud checks run hours too late, etc.

At the same time, making this data available to additional systems, such as an ERP or loyalty application, requires building new, tightly coupled point-to-point integrations with the SFTP server, increasing complexity and maintenance effort, limiting scalability.

Event-Driven Architectures addresses both challenges, it liberates your data. This doesn’t mean replacing the existing components, it means making the data that is already in the organization available in real-time to whoever (system and applications) needs it by putting a broker between the data source (publisher, SFTP server) and the consuming applications (subscribers).

Whenever a CSV file is updated or a new one is dropped, each line item is converted into a JSON message and published as an event, ready for the subscribers to consume it at their pace.

This approach:

✅  Enables real-time data availability
✅  Decouples producers and consumers
✅  Simplifies and accelerates scaling and onboarding/offboarding of applications
✅  Increases reliability through built-in queueing mechanisms in Advanced Event Mesh

Topic Design

For this scenario, we’ll use for the following topic structure:

retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/{region}/{storeId}/{productCategory}/{transactionId}


Advanced Event Mesh supports dynamically subscriptions using wildcards :

  • *   replaces any string of characters
  • >  matches everything beyond it in the hierarchy.

For example :

  • All recorded sales line items (interesting for ERP, data warehouse and loyalty systems) :
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/>
  • Single store dashboard (for store-042):
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/emea/store-042/>
  • Product category filtering (e.g. beverages for replenishment):
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/*/*/beverages/>

The diagram below highlights how everything is connected :

The process

So, let's get started! 🚀

Create a 1K broker service in SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh

Note: if you have already created a broker service, you can skip to step 2 in this section.

  1. Creating the broker
    1. When you open SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh, you open what we refer to as the “Cloud Console” (you can always go back to the cloud console by clicking on the SAP logo on the top left).
    2. Navigate to “Mission Control” -> “Cluster Manager”.
       
    3. There you see an overview of your existing brokers, click “Create Service” on the right top.

    4. Provide at least the following 
      • Service Name
      • Cloud provider and region
      • Service Type: Enterprise
      • Service Class: Broker 1K

    5. Click "Create Service"

  2. Once the service is ready:

    1. Navigate to "Try Me!"

    2. Copy the Client Username and Client Password
      ⚠️Important: you will need this later, so you may want to consider pasting this in a notepad.

       

    3.  Click on “Open Broker Manager”, this opens a page where you can publish messages on topic and queues in your broker and subscribe to these messages as well.
    4. Enter your client password in the connect tab and hit connect (on the left side and right side), now you can try out your broker.⚠️ Remember: keep your client username and password at hand!

Create a temporary SFTP Server

  1. Go to sftpcloud.io 
  2. Click on "Create test SFTP Server".
    This creates a test SFTP Server and it is available for one hour. This should be enough for the duration of this tutorial.
  3. After the creation, you see an overview of:
    • Host
    • Port
    • Username
    • Password
      ⚠️Keep this tab open or copy these credentials to a notepad, we will need them very soon.

Create the SFTP Source Micro-Integration

  1. In the Cloud Console, navigate to "Micro-Integrations"

     

  2.  If this is your first time creating a Micro-Integration, you should click on “Get Started with Micro-Integrations”, in the other case just click “Create Micro-Integration” on the right top.

  3. You are now shown a list of different micro-integrations, let’s find the SFTP Source Micro-Integration. Search for “SFTP” using the search bar on the top left and select the “SFTP Source”.

  4. Now, give the Micro-Integration a name and description, for example: 
    1. Name: sftp-retail-source
    2. Description: Liberates batch sales files from SFTP into real-time event stream
      Click "Next".

  5. Enter a name for the checkpoint queue (e.g. ckpt-sftp-retail) and check the box for “Auto Create Checkpoint queue”. The checkpoint queue will keep track of which files and rows have been published, so the process resumes exactly where it left off after a restart or failure.
    Click “Next”
     
  6. Select your broker.

  7. Scroll down and enter the client username and client password of your broker (copied from step 2 in create the broker section).
    Note: for the purpose of this tutorial we’ll leave the rest as-is. For a real use case, you may want to check the authentication settings.
    Click “Next”.

     

  8. At the summary, click on “Create”.

     

Configure the flow

  1. After the creation of the Micro-Integration, you see a pop-up, in this pop-up click on “Create Flow”, this is where the magic happens.

     

  2. Give your flow a name and description, for example:
    Name: sales-csv-to-lineitem-events
    Description:
    Reads each new or updated sales CSV from the SFTP inbound folder, splits it into individual rows, maps every row to a JSON event, and publishes it to retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/{region}/{storeId}/{productCategory}/{transactionId}

  3. Scroll down to “Source Endpoint” and add your SFTP Server URI in the format:

    sftp://<host>:<port>/upload/retail/sales/inbound

  4. Enter the username and password of the SFTP server created in step 2
  5. Scroll down again, and use the following delimiter file configuration:
    • Event delimiter:  \n
    • Field delimiter: ,
      Note: This means that for each line in the CSV file an event will be created, and that fields are separated with a “,”.

  6. Scroll to Target Endpoint and enter the following Topic Name:
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1
    For our use case, we will work with content-based topics, but since the topic field is mandatory the topic name we add serves as a placeholder.
  7. Click "Next: Error Handling"

  8.  Optionally, you can enable retries and a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) here. Click “Next: Mappings”.

  9. We can now create a mapping, you’ll see a title that says, “No Source Payload”, under this title click on “Import File” and import the Sample JSON.
    After, use the same file for the target payload. We are not going to build a complex mapping in this example, we will focus more on the content-based topics.
    ⚠️Note: Even though we upload CSV files later, the micro-integration automatically converts them to a JSON structure.

  10. After the files are uploaded, link the source and the target fields, they are all the same, make sure you don’t miss one.
     
  11. If you didn’t miss one, you should see a message saying that all target fields are mapped.

Build the content-based topic

  1. Once the mapping is complete, we construct the topic using constants and payload fields. This is how the topic will look like:

    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/{region}/{storeId}/{productCategory}/{transactionId}

  2. Hoover to the left side of your screen, and click on the “+” button next to “Constants”, under “SFTP Source” and add the following constants:
    • First levels of the topic taxonomy:
      Data Type: String
      Constant Value: retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1

    • Separator:
      Data Type: String
      Constant Value: retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1

  3. Drag a line from one of the values that we just created to “Smart Topic Destination” and click on “Add transformation”.

  4. Use the Concatenate function and build a transformation that looks this (use “Add input” to add fields):
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1
    • /
    • region
    • /
    • storeId
    • /
    • productCategory
    • /
    • transactionId
      ⚠️Ensure there are no double slashes (//) or the topic does not end with a slash (/)
  5. Click "Apply", then click "Next: Summary"
  6. Click "Create".
  7. After a few seconds, a page is shown where you see your Micro-Integration and the flow you just created listed, if you made it this far, click on “Deploy”. A pop-up will show to confirm, confirm by clicking “Deploy” once more.

  8. You should now see that the flow is running successfully.

Testing the Micro-Integration

  1. After you have created the micro-integration, go to the cloud console, go to “Cluster Manager” and open your broker.
  2. Go to the “Try Me!” tab, copy the client password and click on “Open Broker Manager”.

  3. Enter the client password on the left side of the screen and hit “Connect” on both the “Publisher” and “Subscriber” side.
     
  4. Subscribe to topics such as:
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/>
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/emea/store-042/>
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/*/*/beverages/>
  5. Now, go to FileZilla Client (or another File Transfer Client) and connect to your SFTP Server.

  6. You should see the following folder structure: /upload/retail/sales/inbound
    This was created by the Micro-Integration flow we created throughout step 3.
    Note: During the setup of the MI, we specified the folder structure. The moment we deployed the flow, it connected to the SFTP Server and looked for the folders. It didn’t find these folders so it created it.
  7. Click through until you are in the folder “inbound”.
  8. Download the sample CSV.
  9. Upload the CSV into the folder “inbound”.

  10. After the upload, go back to the “Try Me!” tab and, you should see the messages flowing in. Feel free to play around with the file and subscriptions.

Troubleshooting

  1. If issues occur:
    1. Check if the SFTP server is still active (expires after 1 hour)
    2. Verify credentials (broker vs SFTP)
    3. Ensure no // in topic construction
    4. Confirm client permissions on the broker (when you didn’t create the broker from scratch)
  2. You can view logs via: 
    Micro-Integrations -> Select the SFTP Source MI we just created -> 3 dots on top right -> View Error Logs

     

Final thoughts

If everything works like expected, congratulations, you just developed an SFTP source micro-integration!

From here, you can create queues subscribing to the topics used in this example and have various applications consuming from these queues.

Hope you enjoyed this blog!
Curious to hear your thoughts; did you like this blog, are there any other topics you would like me to cover,… feel free to share in the comments!

Event-driven architecture SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: Integration between S/4HANA Cloud and Integration Suite with SOAP Outbound service using Output Type
entry.content:
In this blog post we will discuss about Integration between S/4 HANA Cloud to external system via Integration Suite using Output Type as the trigger from S/4 HANA Side. This can be achieved provided there is a standard Outbound service available within any standard SAP Communication scenario. This pattern can also be considered as a good alternative for EDA when there are no standard events available for the business object and in those cases, it can be used instead of going for custom events. The outbound service will use SOAP technology so every time you trigger you will get a XML payload with full data as per API specification.

Advantage – It gives you the full data every time so no need for additional OData calls to S/4 HANA to fetch additional details.

Disadvantage – You do not get delta changes, but you will get the full data every time you trigger. So you can control it from middleware mapping according to your business need.

To demonstrate this use-case today I am using S/4 HANA Public Cloud. I will write a separate blog for S/4 HANA Private Cloud. To configure the triggering using Output Type from S/4 HANA Public cloud we will need to use five standard applications in S/4 HANA.

  1. Maintain Communication Users
  2. Communication Systems
  3. Communication Arrangements
  4. Output Parameter Determination
  5. Assign Recipients to Users (Optional for monitoring purpose)

Step 1 – Create a basic Integration flow with SOAP Sender adapter and deploy it.

In this blog we are only focusing on the part of integration from S/4 HANA Cloud to Integration Suite. The Output Type triggering will be done using a SOAP Outbound service which will eventually send an XML payload. Based on the technology used(API/SFTP) on target side it can be converted or sent as it is after evaluating the capability of the target system to interpret an XML file.

 

Copy the deployed URL and SOAP Address part to be used later.

Step 2 – Activating Communication

We will be using standard communication scenario SAP_COM_0223 to demonstrate the use case here. However, there are other communication scenarios too which has outbound SOAP services available. SAP_COM_0223 has three outbound services, here we will trigger the Outbound delivery.

I have used SAP Business Accelerator Hub to identify my required API for my requirement. API - https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-CE_CO_EDI_DELIVERY_REQUEST_V2_OUT-v2/overview

We need to create a communication arrangement for this standard communication scenario. How to create communication arrangements and its related communication system and communication user that I have covered in one of my previous blogs - API Consumption - S/4 HANA Cloud,Public Edition a... - SAP Community . Also, there are multiple other blogs available in SAP community on this topic.

The hierarchy is – Communication User -> Communication System -> Communication Arrangement

*Additional step for this scenario you have to add a Outbound User  in communication system as in this case our communication direction is S/4 HANA -> External(outgoing)

Also "Inbound only" checkbox should be unchecked in this case.

Most important you have to add the host of your Integration Suite in the field “Host Name” as your S/4 HANA system is communicating with Integration Suite via this communication system.

Once we have created the arrangement, we need to configure the Integration flow URL components in the two fields highlighted in the below screenshot

In the “Path” provide the portion of your deployed URL which is highlighted in the below screenshot

The “Service URL” part is non editable and it is supposed to populated automatically from your assigned communication system when you configure the Host Name in your communication system as shown above

Step 3 – Configuring Output Parameter

Configure the Output Type in the app “Output Parameter Determination”

Open the app –

Configure the standard output type DELIVERY_NOTE for Determination Step = "Output Type"  for “EDI” .

Step 4 – Maintain BP in Communication System

Maintain the business partners in communication system. This is an important step as without this your Output does not gets triggered. The BP for which you are intending to create your Sales Order, and corresponding Outbound delivery needs to be maintained in this section.

Go to “Communication System” app, open the communication system that you are using for the particular communication arrangement and add the BPs under this tab.

Test your Interface using an existing Outbound Delivery or creating a new one.

Here I have triggered output manually but automatic triggering to be configured with appropriate conditions from “Output Parameter Determination”

Go to app – “Change Outbound Delivery”/VL02N and open the delivery number for which you want to trigger the output type.

Go to path –

Menu -> Extras -> Delivery Output -> Output Control

Click on “Add Output” and then click on “Send Output”

Once output shows as “Completed” you can check the message in your Integration flow if it has reached. You can check your Iflow monitoring tab for message received in your Integration Suite–

So once you have the XML payload in your Integration flow, you can do necessary conversion/mapping or send as it is to target using API or SFTP whichever capability your target system supports.

**Additional tips -  For monitoring you can create a recipient for this interface from the application – Assign Recipients to Users with names space and recipient name as below -

You can monitor SOAP outbound messages from the applications like –

Message Monitoring for Integration Experts

Message Monitoring

entry.title: Using the AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite to Generate Integration Incident Summaries
entry.content:
Introduction

When an integration fails, support teams often need to interpret technical error messages before they can understand the root cause and decide on the next action. For experienced integration developers, this may be straightforward, but for first-line support or business users, the message can be difficult to translate into something actionable.

In this mini-blog, a simple proof of concept shows how SAP Integration Suite and a Large Language Model (LLM) can transform a raw integration error payload into a structured incident summary by using the built-in AI Adapter in Cloud Integration.

For adapter capabilities, configuration details, and supported variants, refer to SAP Help and existing other SAP blog post by Shashank_K.

Scenario

In real integration scenarios, an incident often contains only a few technical fields, such as:

  • MPL ID.
  • Timestamp.
  • Endpoint.
  • Error message.

Instead of manually interpreting these values every time, the flow sends the incident details to an LLM and asks it to generate a structured summary containing:

  • Incident summary.
  • Root cause.
  • Severity.
  • Recommendation.

This helps reduce analysis time and makes incident triage easier for support teams.

Solution Overview

The integration flow receives the incident payload through an HTTP endpoint and forwards the content to an LLM using the AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. The AI Adapter for SAP Integration Suite facilitates and accelerates connectivity to Generative AI Hub (SAP AI Core) and other AI providers and is available in AICore-GenAIHub and Custom-OpenAI-REST variants. The LLM returns a structured incident summary that can be displayed, logged, or forwarded to another support process.

Client -> Cloud Integration -> AI Adapter -> LLM (SAP AI Core - GenAI Hub) -> Structured Incident Summary

It is worth noting for this exact scenario, SAP Integration Suite already offers AI-assisted analysis for failed message processing logs, where the AI assistant can identify the main error cause and propose a solution. For more details, refer to SAP Help: AI-assisted Error Resolution. Also note: AI features are accessible only with the Premium and Enhanced Editions. These are provided as a free promotion through September 2026 and will be commercialized later as AI features using AI Units. For more information on availability of these AI features across SAP BTP regions, see 3463620.

Building the Integration Flow

Prerequisites:
This mini-blog assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite and have already provisioned SAP AI Core with GenAI Hub enabled. If you are new to SAP AI Core, you can start with the SAP Developers mission to set up SAP AI Core on SAP Business Technology Platform and then explore concepts such as orchestration and the foundation models that make up SAP AI Core: Set Up SAP AI Core in SAP BTP.

The Integration Flow in my demo scenario contains the following steps:

  • HTTPS Sender Adapter.
  • JSON to XML Converter.
  • Content Modifier.
  • Request-Reply step.
  • AI Adapter.
  • JSON to XML Converter.
  • Content Modifier.
  • HTTPS Response.

Here is how my Integration Flow looks like. The JSON to XML Converter step transforms the incoming JSON payload into XML format so it can be processed more easily by downstream steps in the iFlow, since the payload is sent as JSON from Bruno, a REST client tool.

Create the exchange properties below to map the values that will be used later in the message flow between Request Reply 1 and the Receiver.

On Message Flow between Request Reply 1 to Receiver, fill-in the connection details (you can get the details from SAP AI Core > Service Key).

Before filling the OAuth2 Client Credentials field, first create the OAuth2 Client Credentials in Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Manage Security Material. Here are example configurations.

In my demo scenario, for the Processing details configuration, I'm using the following:
 

Operation

Orchestration - Completion

Orchestration - Deployment ID

$header.deployment_id

AI Resource Group

Default

Request Payload Source

UI Configurable

Model

Gemini-2.5-flash

Role

Assistant

Input

You are an SAP Integration Suite support assistant. Analyze ONLY the provided incident information (Timestamp=${property.timestamp}, Endpoint=${property.endpoint}, Error Message=${property.errorMessage}) and return ONLY: Summary, Root Cause, Severity (Low|Medium|High), Recommendation. Max 100 words. No explanations, no markdown, no placeholders, no extra text.

Request Headers

*

As I don't want to hard-code the Orchestration - Deployment ID, so I'm passing it in my HTTP headers with key name deployment_id.

Refer to this SAP Help documentation page, if you need to do variant of configuration such as using Exchange Body as Request Payload Source allows you to specify required structure and values.
Note: the input content should be 1 liner, otherwise you will get an error upon deployment.

On Content Modifier 2, map the result into aiResult in Exchange Property:

Finally in Message Body return the following JSON as final output:

Testing the Flow

You can test the flow using Bruno or any REST client tool (i.e. Postman or cUrl) by sending the incident payload to the HTTP endpoint.

Input payload:

{
	  "mplId": "AGo046SjNI9VZ4l",
	  "timestamp": "2026-06-22T10:00:00Z",
	  "endpoint": "/api/orders",
	  "errorMessage": "OAuth token validation failed"
}

Response payload:
 

{
	"incidentAnalysis": "Summary: API calls to /api/orders are failing due to OAuth token validation failure.
	Root Cause: Invalid or expired OAuth token used for authentication to the /api/orders endpoint.
	Severity: Medium
	Recommendation: Verify current OAuth token validity, expiration, and scopes. Regenerate or refresh the token as needed."
}

Example from Bruno:

Key Takeaways

SAP Integration Suite can do more than move data between systems — with the AI Adapter, it becomes easier to integrate with an LLM and turn raw technical errors into structured summaries. This helps support teams understand failures faster, identify likely root causes, and take the right next action.

entry.title: SAP Expert Lecture: Accelerating Hybrid Integrations with SAP Integration Suite on Microsoft Azure
entry.content:
Are you ready to take your hybrid integration skills to the next level? We are excited to announce a new SAP Expert Lecture: Hybrid Integrations with SAP Integration Suite on Microsoft Azure.

About This Course

This SAP Expert Lecture dives deep into hybrid integration using SAP Integration Suite on Microsoft Azure. It will be presented by a team of seasoned SAP professionals from Product Management for SAP Integration Suite, joined by experts from Microsoft. Their combined experience brings a rare mix of product depth and real-world deployment knowledge to every module.

Who Should Join

  • Integration Architects — who design and govern hybrid integration landscapes across cloud and on-premise systems
  • SAP BTP / Integration Suite Developers — who build and maintain integration flows and APIs
  • Cloud & Platform Engineers — who work with Kubernetes, Microsoft Azure, or AKS in an SAP context
  • IT Decision-Makers & Solution Architects — who evaluate hybrid integration strategies and infrastructure options

What You'll Learn

  • Hybrid Integration with Edge Integration Cell: understand the architecture and deployment model
  • Microsoft Azure for SAP Applications and Kubernetes: navigate AKS setup and configuration
  • Prerequisites and Best Practices: avoid common pitfalls and deploy with confidence

How It Works

Start date: Tuesday, August 18, 2026

Format: Self-paced video lectures with downloadable learning materials

Community: Discussion forum open until September 17, 2026 — engage directly with the experts

Completion: Pass the final quiz to earn a Record of Achievement

Ready to deepen your hybrid integration expertise?
Explore and register here

Kind regards,
Britta Radicke-Schlinsog
(SAP Expert Lectures Team)

entry.title: SAP CI Extension: Adding Horizontal Layouts to Message Mappings
entry.content:
Introduction

If you have worked with SAP Cloud Integration, you probably appreciate the "Arrange Horizontally" button for iFlows. You click it, and your cluttered integration steps instantly line up in a clean, horizontal flow. Under the hood, SAP uses Eclipse Layout Kernel (ELK) to handle this stuff.

But for some reason, that neat layout feature is nowhere to be found within Message Mappings. Instead, you are left manually dragging expression blocks around, trying to keep a complex mapping from looking like a bowl of spaghetti. Some people LOVE to throw in complex mapping expressions onto the canvas with no semblance of order in the layout. If you are one of them, your teammates have my condolences.

Since SAP already uses ELK for iFlows, and message mapping expressions are inherently graph nodes, it should be possible to "auto layout" mapping expressions as well. While SAP may or may not ever bring this feature into their official roadmap, we don't have to wait around for a future release cycle. We can patch it ourselves.

After digging through browser XHR requests, tracking DOM mutations, and exploring SAP’s internal web rendering, I figured out roughly how to do this and bring this "auto layout" to SAP Message Mapping expressions.

 

The Internal Pipeline

To change how the mapping looks, you first have to understand how SAP handles the data behind the scenes. When you open a message mapping, SAP fetches your <mappingName>.mmap file which is nothing but the ELK json of your current mapping expressions.

Based on my analysis of the UI scripts, the internal rendering pipeline might look something like this:

<mappingName>.mmap ELK JSON
↓
TransformationModel
↓
Marshaller
↓
ExpressionModel
↓
Galileo (UI Framework)
↓
Raphael (SVG Library)

SAP converts the raw ELK JSON into a TransformationModel to track changes, then a Marshaller deserializes it into an ExpressionModel. Finally, SAP uses Galileo and Raphael to render the nodes and connecting lines on your page.

Because the underlying data structure relies entirely on coordinate based graph nodes, we don't need to fight the framework. We can intercept the model, recalculate the positions ourselves using elkjs, update the UI, and save the clean layout back to SAP.

 

Intercepting the Mapping Data

By reading the target package name and integration flow name from the current browser URL, we can dynamically look up the reg IDs using SAP's standard OData API:

  • Find the Package ID:
GET /odata/1.0/workspace.svc/ContentEntities.ContentPackages('${packageName}')?$format=json
  • Find the Artifact ID:
GET /odata/1.0/workspace.svc/ContentEntities.ContentPackages('${packageName}')/Artifacts?$format=json
  • Once we have these, we get the current ELK json by:
GET /api/1.0/workspace/${packageRegId}/artifacts/${flowRegId}/resources/<mappingName>.mmap?modelType=MappingModelTO&path=src%2Fmain%2Fresources%2Fmapping

To modify the layout, we need access to SAP’s internal mapping controller. Because this controller isn't exposed globally, we can write a deep walk utility that scans the window object to find the active instance by looking for a unique property like _mappingValidator.

Once we find the controller, we can render the "prettified" mapping JSON data directly into the application state, more on that later.

function findMappingController() {
  const visited = new WeakSet();

  function walk(obj) {
    if (!obj || typeof obj !== "object") return null;
    if (visited.has(obj)) return null;
    visited.add(obj);
    try {
      if (obj._mappingValidator) return obj;
      for (const key in obj) {
        try {
          const result = walk(obj[key]);
          if (result) return result;
        } catch {}
      }
    } catch {}
    return null;
  }

  let result = null;
  for (const key in window) {
    try {
      result = walk(window[key]);
      if (result) break;
    } catch {}
  }

  window.__activeMappingController = result;
  return !!result;
}

function extractMappingData() {
  const c = window.__activeMappingController;
  if (!c) return null;
  const t = c._mappingValidator._transformation;
  return t.getJSONData ? t.getJSONData() : JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(t));
}

 

Computing the Clean Layout with ELK

Now that have the "current" JSON model, we need to restructure it into a format that elkjs understands. We map destinations, source nodes, and functions to ELK nodes with fixed dimensions, then create edges between them.

We configure the ELK layout options to use the layered algorithm and set the direction to RIGHT to mirror the exact horizontal aesthetic SAP uses for the iflow horizontal alignment.

import ELK from "elkjs/lib/elk.bundled.js";

const elk = new ELK();

export async function prettifyModel(model) {
  function collectGraphElements(mappings) {
    const nodes = new Map();
    const edges = [];

    function visit(ref, parentId = null) {
      if (!ref) return;

      const graphicalNode = ref.graphicalNode;
      const graphicalFunction = ref.graphicalFunction;

      if (graphicalNode) {
        const id = graphicalNode.expressionDetails.objectId;

        if (!nodes.has(id)) {
          nodes.set(id, {
            id,
            width: 140,
            height: 40,
            ref: graphicalNode,
          });
        }

        if (parentId) {
          edges.push({
            id: crypto.randomUUID(),
            sources: [id],
            targets: [parentId],
          });
        }
        return;
      }

      if (graphicalFunction) {
        const id = graphicalFunction.expressionDetails.objectId;

        if (!nodes.has(id)) {
          nodes.set(id, {
            id,
            width: 120,
            height: 50,
            ref: graphicalFunction,
          });
        }

        if (parentId) {
          edges.push({
            id: crypto.randomUUID(),
            sources: [id],
            targets: [parentId],
          });
        }

        for (const pin of graphicalFunction.expressionDetails.pinins || []) {
          const expr = pin.pinout?.expressionReference;
          visit(expr, id);
        }
      }
    }

    for (const mapping of mappings) {
      const dst = mapping.destination.expressionDetails;
      const dstId = dst.objectId;

      nodes.set(dstId, {
        id: dstId,
        width: 160,
        height: 40,
        ref: mapping.destination,
      });

      for (const pin of dst.pinins || []) {
        visit(pin.pinout?.expressionReference, dstId);
      }
    }

    return {
      nodes: [...nodes.values()],
      edges,
    };
  }

  function createElkGraph(graph) {
    return {
      id: "root",
      layoutOptions: {
        "elk.algorithm": "layered",
        "elk.layered.unnecessaryBendpoints": "true",
        "elk.direction": "RIGHT",
        "elk.layered.spacing.nodeNodeBetweenLayers": "5",
        "elk.spacing.nodeNode": "5",
      },
      children: graph.nodes.map((n) => ({
        id: n.id,
        width: n.width,
        height: n.height,
      })),
      edges: graph.edges,
    };
  }

  async function prettify(mappingJson) {
    const graph = collectGraphElements(mappingJson.mappings);
    const elkGraph = createElkGraph(graph);
    const result = await elk.layout(elkGraph);

    const positions = new Map(
      result.children.map((c) => [c.id, { x: c.x, y: c.y }])
    );

    for (const node of graph.nodes) {
      const pos = positions.get(node.id);
      if (!pos) continue;

      node.ref.expressionDetails.position.x = Math.round(pos.x);
      node.ref.expressionDetails.position.y = Math.round(pos.y);
    }

    return mappingJson;
  }

  return prettify(model);
}

 

Pushing the Changes Back to the UI

Once ELK returns the computed x and y coordinates, we inject them back into the active transformation model and force Galileo to re render the canvas view.

function applyPrettifiedData(data) {
  const c = window.__activeMappingController;
  if (!c) return false;
  const t = c._mappingValidator._transformation;
  t.setJSONMappings(data.mappings);
  c._refreshUI();
  c._showGraphicalExpression(t.getMapping(c._sCurrentMappingId), false);
  return true;
}

 

Saving the Layout to SAP

Updating the UI canvas allows you to see the clean layout, but we also want this to be a persistent. To do that we must save the current prettified ELK JSON to the backend server so they remain intact the next time anyone opens the mapping artifact.

With the reg IDs already known, we can push the updated mapping file back to the cloud workspace using a standard PUT request.

Note: The API endpoint does not accept raw JSON payloads here. You must compress your JSON data using gzip before passing it along.

PUT /api/1.0/workspace/${packageRegId}/artifacts/${flowRegId}/resources/<mappingName>.mmap?modelType=MappingModelTO&path=src%2Fmain%2Fresources%2Fmapping&name=<mappingName>.mmap&type=messagemapping

To trigger the layout update smoothly, you will need to append a custom HTML button onto SAP's mapping page to call these functions sequentially. Add a simple loading overlay while ELK does it's thing and the API updates, and you will have a fully functioning, automatic layout button for your message mappings.

You can package these code blocks into a custom browser extension or inject them via a user script manager like Tampermonkey. If you use existing community tools like the CPI Helper extension, this would make for a great pull request idea to elevate the experience for everyone. If you did everything right, you should be able to get the results as I did:

entry.title: Upcoming Migration Webinars: Accelerate your transition from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
If you're still running your integrations on SAP PI/PO, you already know what's coming. On-premise integration has had its day - and the window to act is now!

To help you move forward with confidence, we're hosting a two migration webinars dedicated to one thing: getting you from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite without the guesswork.

Why now?

SAP PI/PO served its purpose. But the integration landscape has changed. Modern enterprise architecture demands cloud-native flexibility, real-time connectivity, and the ability to scale fast - across SAP and non-SAP systems alike. SAP Integration Suite delivers exactly that: a single, unified platform that consolidates your entire integration landscape and opens the door to AI-powered automation. Staying on PI/PO isn't standing still. It's falling behind.

What you'll learn:

SAP PI/PO Migration — The Big Picture
Where does your migration journey start, and where does it lead? We'll set the stage with a concise overview of the move from PI/PO and what success looks like.

Assessment Tool - See It in Action
Get introduced to the migration assessment capability and watch a live demo of its key features. Know where you stand before you make a single move.

Migration Tool - How It Actually Works
A practical walkthrough of the migration tooling - what it does, how it supports your transition, and what to expect when you use it.

What's New in the Community
Latest resources, documentation updates, and community contributions worth knowing about. Stay current without having to dig for it.

What's Coming Next
A snapshot of the migration roadmap - upcoming milestones, priorities, and planned enhancements. So you can plan ahead, not just react.

Q&A
Open floor. Bring your questions, edge cases, and real-world scenarios. We'll tackle them live.

 

How to join?

Interested? Great! Then make sure you register for one of following migration Webinars via the links below:

 

  • Session in English: 14th of July 2026  [Register here]
  • Session in Chinese: 16th of July 2026 [Register here]

 

See you there!

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics, Queues and Initial Setup
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh — Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture & Setup

A 3-part hands-on series on SAP Event Mesh: Event-Driven Architecture, Pub-Sub, and CPI integration.

Series at a glance
Part 1 (this post):  EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key

Part 2: Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume)
Part 3:  Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet

Environment used in this series: SAP BTP·

Most SAP integration developers learn point-to-point patterns first RFC, SOAP, OData. They work, but they tightly couple systems together. The moment you need a *second* system to react to the same business event, you end up duplicating calls and adding brittle dependencies.

SAP Event Mesh flips that model. In this first post we'll cover the architectural concepts you need, then walk through provisioning Event Mesh end to end so it's ready for the integration work in Part 2.

1. Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — Core Concepts

1.1 What is Event-Driven Architecture?

Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a software design pattern in which systems communicate by producing and consuming events. Instead of calling each other directly (RFC, SOAP, OData), systems publish events to a central hub, and any interested system subscribes to those events.

Why EDA over traditional integration:

- Loose coupling — systems don't need to know about each other
- Scalability — multiple subscribers can consume the same event
- Resilience — if a subscriber is down, events wait safely in the queue
- Real-time processing — events are handled as they happen

1.2 How Event Mesh works — the Pub-Sub flow

   

 

 

 



Flow explained 

1. S/4HANA Cloud (the publisher) sends an event to a topic (e.g. `CustomerMaster`).
2. Event Mesh receives the event and routes it to every queue subscribed to that topic.
3. Each subscriber system (Shopify, C4C, 3PL) has its own queue.
4. Consumer systems pull messages from their own queue, independently of each other.

The key insight: one publish can fan out to many consumers, and each consumer reads at its own pace.

1.3 The three types of events

Event Type Description SAP Example

Notification EventA lightweight event that notifies consumers that something has happened. It typically contains minimal information and prompts consumers to retrieve additional data if required.A Business Partner is created in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, triggering a notification event.
Data EventAn event that carries the actual business data, allowing consumers to process the information without making an additional API call.A Customer Master event containing customer details such as name, address, and contact information.
Transaction EventAn event representing the completion of an entire business transaction, often including related business objects and line-item details.A Sales Order Confirmation event containing the order header, customer information, and all line items.



1.4 Key terminology

Term Description Example

TopicA logical communication channel where publishers send events. Every queue subscribed to a topic receives a copy of the published event. Topics typically follow a namespace-based naming convention.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/CustomerMaster
QueueA durable message store dedicated to a specific consumer. Each consuming application (such as Shopify or SAP Sales Cloud) should have its own queue to process messages independently.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
NamespaceA hierarchical prefix used to organize and manage topics and queues. It ensures consistent naming and controls which resources can publish or subscribe to events.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata
PublisherThe application or service that generates and publishes events to a topic. Publishers do not need to know who consumes the events.SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), Custom Applications
Subscriber (Consumer)The application or service that receives events by reading messages from its subscribed queue. Each consumer processes messages independently.Shopify, SAP Sales Cloud, 3PL System
Pub-Sub ModelA messaging pattern where publishers send events to topics, and subscribers receive them through their own queues without direct communication between systems. This enables loose coupling and scalability.One Customer Master event consumed by multiple downstream systems
SAP Event MeshSAP's managed messaging service that routes events from publishers to subscribed queues using the Publish-Subscribe architecture. It provides reliable asynchronous communication between applications.Routes a Customer Master event from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to Shopify, SAP Sales Cloud, and a 3PL system
EMIS (Event Mesh Integration Suite)The embedded SAP Event Mesh service available within SAP Integration Suite. It provides the same messaging capabilities as standalone SAP Event Mesh but is managed directly through Integration Suite.Event Mesh accessed from SAP Integration Suite

 

2. SAP Event Mesh – Initial Setup

In this section, we'll provision an SAP Event Mesh instance, create queues, configure queue subscriptions, and generate a service key. By the end of these steps, your Event Mesh environment will be ready for publishing and consuming events in Part 2 of this series.

💡EMIS vs. Standalone SAP Event Mesh

Event Mesh Integration Suite (EMIS) is the embedded Event Mesh service available within SAP Integration Suite. It provides the same core messaging capabilities as the standalone SAP Event Mesh service, including topics, queues, subscriptions, and asynchronous message routing.

The primary difference is how you access and manage the service:

  • Standalone SAP Event Mesh is provisioned and managed directly from the SAP BTP Cockpit.

  • EMIS is accessed and managed through the SAP Integration Suite application.

For the concepts covered in this series—such as topics, queues, publishers, subscribers, and service keys—the configuration and behavior are essentially the same regardless of which deployment option you use.

Step 1 – Create an SAP Event Mesh Instance

The first step is to provision an SAP Event Mesh instance in your SAP BTP subaccount. During instance creation, you'll define the namespace, messaging capabilities, and authorization rules that govern how publishers and subscribers interact with Event Mesh.

Follow these steps:

  1. Sign in to the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to your Subaccount.

  2. From the left navigation pane, select Services → Service Marketplace.

  3. Search for Event Mesh and select the service.

  4. Click Create (or Create Instance, depending on your BTP version).

  5. Choose the appropriate Service Plan.

  6. In the Service Instance Parameters section, paste the following service descriptor JSON.

  7. Click Create to provision the Event Mesh instance.

Service Descriptor

// {
  "resources": { "units": "10" },
  "options": { "management": true, "messagingrest": true, "messaging": true },
  "rules": {
    "topicRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    },
    "queueRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    }
  },
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "emname": "S4_RudhTesting",
  "namespace": "S4HC/Testing/Masterdata"
}

💡Tip

The namespace acts as the root path for all topics and queues created within your Event Mesh instance. For example, if your namespace is:

S4HC/Testing/Masterdata

then every topic and queue should begin with this prefix, ensuring a consistent naming convention and simplifying access control.


Step 2 — Create queues

Each consuming system needs its own queue. In the Event Mesh dashboard:

1. Open the Event Mesh dashboard .

 


2. Go to the  Queues tab.
3. Click **Create Queue** for each consumer:
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify` — for Shopify integration
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C` — for C4C / SAP Sales Cloud
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl` — for the 3PL / logistics system

💡 Best Practice

Queue names should always begin with your configured namespace to maintain a consistent naming convention and simplify resource management.

Each consuming application should have its own dedicated queue. When an event is published to a topic, SAP Event Mesh delivers a copy of the message to every queue subscribed to that topic. This allows each consumer to process the event independently, without affecting other subscribers.

 

Step 3 – Subscribe Queues to Topics

After creating the queues, the next step is to subscribe each queue to the topic(s) from which it should receive messages.

Follow these steps:

  1. Open the SAP Event Mesh Dashboard.

  2. Navigate to the Queues tab and select the required queue.

  3. Click the Subscriptions (or Queue Subscriptions) icon.

  4. Choose Add Subscription and enter the topic name.

  5. Save the subscription.

Example

Queue Subscribed Topic

S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/ShopifyS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4CS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C/CustomerMaster
S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3PLS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3PL/CustomerMaster

⚠️ Important

Topic names are case-sensitive. For example, CustomerMaster and customermaster are treated as two different topics.

Ensure that the topic name configured by the publisher exactly matches the topic subscribed to by the queue. Even a small difference in letter casing or spelling will prevent messages from being delivered.

💡 Best Practice

Establish a consistent topic naming convention early in your project and reuse it across SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), and SAP Event Mesh. Consistent naming reduces configuration errors and makes troubleshooting much easier.

Step 4 – Create a Service Key

A Service Key contains the connection details and authentication credentials required for external applications and integration flows to connect securely to SAP Event Mesh.

The generated JSON includes information such as the OAuth 2.0 client credentials, token endpoint, messaging endpoints, and management API URLs that are used by applications like SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), Postman, or custom applications.

Follow these steps to create a Service Key:

  1. In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Event Mesh service instance.

  2. Open the Service Keys section.

  3. Click Create Service Key.

  4. Enter a meaningful name for the service key (for example, EventMesh_CPI_Key).

  5. Click Create.

  6. Once the service key has been generated, open it and download or copy the JSON. This JSON contains all the credentials required to authenticate and connect to your Event Mesh instance.

Service Key – Important Fields

Field Purpose

namespaceDefines the namespace prefix used for topics and queues.
management.uriREST endpoint for managing queues, subscriptions, and other Event Mesh resources.
management.oauth2.tokenendpointOAuth 2.0 token endpoint used to obtain access tokens for the Management API.
messaging[httprest].uriREST endpoint used to publish messages over HTTP.
messaging[amqp10ws].uriAMQP over WebSocket endpoint used by applications such as SAP Cloud Integration.
uaa.clientidOAuth 2.0 Client ID used for authentication.
uaa.clientsecretOAuth 2.0 Client Secret used to obtain access tokens.

🔒 Security Best Practice

Treat the Service Key as a sensitive credential. Never publish or share your Client ID, Client Secret, tenant-specific OAuth endpoints, or downloaded Service Key JSON in public repositories, blogs, or documentation.
